2605.18565.title: LongMINT: Evaluating Memory under Multi-Target Interference in Long-Horizon Agent Systems
2605.18565.paper_summary: Real-world agents operate over long and evolving horizons, where information is repeatedly updated and may interfere across memories, requiring accurate recall and aggregated reasoning over multiple pieces of information. However, existing benchmarks focus on static, independent recall and fail to capture these dynamic interactions between evolving memories. In this paper, we study how current memory-augmented agents perform in realistic, interference-heavy, long-horizon settings across diverse domains and question types. We introduce LongMINT (Long-Horizon Memory under INTerference), a benchmark featuring (1) long, highly interconnected contexts with frequently updated information that induces substantial interference, (2) diverse domains (state tracking, multi-turn dialogue, Wikipedia revisions, and GitHub commits), enabling evaluation of domain generalization, and (3) diverse question types that assess robustness to interference, including (i) single-target recall tasks requiring retrieval of a specific target from long contexts, and (ii) multi-target aggregation tasks requiring reasoning over multiple relevant pieces of information. Overall, LongMINT has 15.6k question-answering pairs over long-horizon contexts averaging 138.8k tokens and extending up to 1.8M tokens per instance. We evaluate 7 representative systems, including vanilla long-context LLMs, RAG, and memory-augmented agent frameworks. Across all systems, we observe consistently low performance (avg. 27.9% accuracy), especially on questions requiring aggregated reasoning over multiple pieces of evidence. Our analysis shows that performance is primarily limited by retrieval and memory construction. Furthermore, current memory systems struggle to recall and reason over earlier facts that are later revised or interfered with by subsequent context, with performance degrading as the number of intervening updates increases.
2605.18565.paper_summary_zh: 真實世界的代理在長期且不斷演變的範疇中運作，資訊會不斷更新，並可能在記憶之間相互干擾，這需要準確的回憶和對多個資訊片段的綜合推理。<lb/>然而，現有的基準測試專注於靜態、獨立的回憶，無法捕捉這些演變記憶之間的動態互動。<lb/>在本文中，我們研究當前記憶增強代理在現實的、干擾重的、長期範疇設置中在不同領域和問題類型下的表現。<lb/>我們介紹了 LongMINT（長期記憶下的干擾），這是一個基準測試，具有 (1) 長且高度互聯的上下文，包含經常更新的資訊，導致顯著的干擾，(2) 多樣的領域（狀態追蹤、多輪對話、維基百科修訂和 GitHub 提交），使得領域泛化的評估成為可能，以及 (3) 多樣的問題類型，評估對干擾的穩健性，包括 (i) 單目標回憶任務，要求從長上下文中檢索特定目標，以及 (ii) 多目標聚合任務，要求對多個相關資訊片段進行推理。<lb/>總體而言，LongMINT 擁有 15.6k 的問題-回答對，涵蓋平均 138.8k 字元的長期上下文，並延伸至每個實例最多 1.8M 字元。<lb/>我們評估了 7 個代表性系統，包括普通的長上下文 LLM、RAG 和記憶增強代理框架。<lb/>在所有系統中，我們觀察到一致的低表現（平均 27.9% 的準確率），特別是在需要對多個證據片段進行聚合推理的問題上。<lb/>我們的分析顯示，表現主要受到檢索和記憶構建的限制。此外，當前的記憶系統在回憶和推理早期事實時面臨困難，這些事實在後續上下文中被修訂或干擾，隨著干擾更新的數量增加，表現逐漸下降。
2605.18565.author: Hyunji Lee et.al.
2605.18565.authors: Hyunji Lee, Justin Chih-Yao Chen, Joykirat Singh, Zaid Khan, Elias Stengel-Eskin, Mohit Bansal
